attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Upgrading
3	description: Learn how to upgrade your Next.js application to the latest version or canary.
4	related:
5	  title: Version guides
6	  description: See the version guides for in-depth upgrade instructions.
7	  links:
8	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-16
9	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-15
10	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-14
11	---
12	
13	## Latest version
14	
15	To update to the latest version of Next.js, you can use the `upgrade` command:
16	
17	```bash package="pnpm"
18	pnpm next upgrade
19	```
20	
21	```bash package="npm"
22	npx next upgrade
23	```
24	
25	```bash package="yarn"
26	yarn next upgrade
27	```
28	
29	```bash package="bun"
30	bunx next upgrade
31	```
32	
33	Next.js 15 and earlier do not support the `upgrade` command and need to use a separate package instead:
34	
35	```bash filename="Terminal"
36	npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
37	```
38	
39	If you prefer to upgrade manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:
40	
41	```bash package="pnpm"
42	pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
43	```
44	
45	```bash package="npm"
46	npm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
47	```
48	
49	```bash package="yarn"
50	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
51	```
52	
53	```bash package="bun"
54	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
55	```
56	
57	## Canary version
58	
59	To update to the latest canary, make sure you're on the latest version of Next.js and everything is working as expected. Then, run the following command:
60	
61	```bash package="pnpm"
62	pnpm add next@canary
63	```
64	
65	```bash package="npm"
66	npm i next@canary
67	```
68	
69	```bash package="yarn"
70	yarn add next@canary
71	```
72	
73	```bash package="bun"
74	bun add next@canary
75	```
76	
77	### Features available in canary
78	
79	The following features are currently available in canary:
80	
81	**Authentication**:
82	
83	- [`forbidden`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden)
84	- [`unauthorized`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unauthorized)
85	- [`forbidden.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/forbidden)
86	- [`unauthorized.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/unauthorized)
87	- [`authInterrupts`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/authInterrupts)
88

1 --- 2 title: Layouts and Pages 3 description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11 - app/api-reference/components/link 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13 --- 14 15 Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16 17 ## Creating a page 18 19 A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20 21 28 29 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30 export default function Page() { 31 return

Hello Next.js!

32 } 33 ``` 34 35 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36 export default function Page() { 37 return

Hello Next.js!

38 } 39 ``` 40 41 ## Creating a layout 42 43 A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68
{children}
69 70 71 ) 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76 export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77 return ( 78 79 80 {/* Layout UI */} 81 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82
{children}
83 84 85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94 95 - `/` (Root Segment) 96 - `blog` (Segment) 97 - `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98 99 In Next.js: 100 101 - **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102 - **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103 104 To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105 106

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Route Handlers
3	nav_title: Route Handlers
4	description: Learn how to use Route Handlers
5	related:
6	  title: API Reference
7	  description: Learn more about Route Handlers
8	  links:
9	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/route
10	    - app/guides/backend-for-frontend
11	---
12	
13	## Route Handlers
14	
15	Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs.
16	
17	<Image
18	  alt="Route.js Special File"
19	  srcLight="/docs/light/route-special-file.png"
20	  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-special-file.png"
21	  width="1600"
22	  height="444"
23	/>
24	
25	> **Good to know**: Route Handlers are only available inside the `app` directory. They are the equivalent of [API Routes](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/api-routes) inside the `pages` directory meaning you **do not** need to use API Routes and Route Handlers together.
26	
27	### Convention
28	
29	Route Handlers are defined in a [`route.js|ts` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) inside the `app` directory:
30	
31	```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher
32	export async function GET(request: Request) {}
33	```
34	
35	```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher
36	export async function GET(request) {}
37	```
38	
39	Route Handlers can be nested anywhere inside the `app` directory, similar to `page.js` and `layout.js`. But there **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route segment level as `page.js`.
40	
41	### Supported HTTP Methods
42	
43	The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. If an unsupported method is called, Next.js will return a `405 Method Not Allowed` response.
44	
45	### Extended `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` APIs
46	
47	In addition to supporting the native [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs, Next.js extends them with [`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) and [`NextResponse`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response) to provide convenient helpers for advanced use cases.
48	
49	### Caching
50	
51	Route Handlers are not cached by default. You can, however, opt into caching for `GET` methods. Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached. To cache a `GET` method, use a [route config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file.
52	
53	```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher
54	export const dynamic = 'force-static'
55	
56	export async function GET() {
57	  const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', {
58	    headers: {
59	      'Content-Type': 'application/json',
60	      'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY,
61	    },
62	  })
63	  const data = await res.json()
64	
65	  return Response.json({ data })
66	}
67	```
68	
69	```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher
70	export const dynamic = 'force-static'
71	
72	export async function GET() {
73	  const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', {
74	    headers: {
75	      'Content-Type': 'application/json',
76	      'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY,
77	    },
78	  })
79	  const data = await res.json()
80	
81	  return Response.json({ data })
82	}
83	```
84	
85	> **Good to know**: Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached, even if they are placed alongside a `GET` method that is cached, in the same file.
86	
87	#### With Cache Components
88	
89	When [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same model as normal UI routes in your application. They run at request time by default, can be prerendered when they don't access dynamic or runtime…(+4916)

1 --- 2 title: Error Handling 3 description: Learn how to display expected errors and handle uncaught exceptions. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/functions/redirect 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/error 10 - app/api-reference/functions/not-found 11 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/not-found 12 --- 13 14 Errors can be divided into two categories: [expected errors](#handling-expected-errors) and [uncaught exceptions](#handling-uncaught-exceptions). This page will walk you through how you can handle these errors in your Next.js application. 15 16 ## Handling expected errors 17 18 Expected errors are those that can occur during the normal operation of the application, such as those from [server-side form validation](/docs/app/guides/forms) or failed requests. These errors should be handled explicitly and returned to the client. 19 20 ### Server Functions 21 22 You can use the [`useActionState`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useActionState) hook to handle expected errors in [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions). 23 24 For these errors, avoid using `try`/`catch` blocks and throw errors. Instead, model expected errors as return values. 25 26 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 27 'use server' 28 29 export async function createPost(prevState: any, formData: FormData) { 30 const title = formData.get('title') 31 const content = formData.get('content') 32 33 const res = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/posts', { 34 method: 'POST', 35 body: { title, content }, 36 }) 37 const json = await res.json() 38 39 if (!res.ok) { 40 return { message: 'Failed to create post' } 41 } 42 } 43 ``` 44 45 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 46 'use server' 47 48 export async function createPost(prevState, formData) { 49 const title = formData.get('title') 50 const content = formData.get('content') 51 52 const res = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/posts', { 53 method: 'POST', 54 body: { title, content }, 55 }) 56 const json = await res.json() 57 58 if (!res.ok) { 59 return { message: 'Failed to create post' } 60 } 61 } 62 ``` 63 64 You can pass your action to the `useActionState` hook and use the returned `state` to display an error message. 65 66 ```tsx filename="app/ui/form.tsx" highlight={11,19} switcher 67 'use client' 68 69 import { useActionState } from 'react' 70 import { createPost } from '@/app/actions' 71 72 const initialState = { 73 message: '', 74 } 75 76 export function Form() { 77 const [state, formAction, pending] = useActionState(createPost, initialState) 78 79 return ( 80
81 82 83 84

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Metadata and OG images
3	description: Learn how to add metadata to your pages and create dynamic OG images.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the Metadata APIs mentioned in this page.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/image-response
11	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata
12	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons
13	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image
14	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots
15	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap
16	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots
17	---
18	
19	The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include:
20	
21	1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata)
22	2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata)
23	3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images).
24	
25	With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `<head>` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools.
26	
27	The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components.
28	
29	## Default fields
30	
31	There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata:
32	
33	- The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website.
34	- The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices.
35	
36	```html
37	<meta charset="utf-8" />
38	<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
39	```
40	
41	The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)).
42	
43	## Static metadata
44	
45	To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route:
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher
48	import type { Metadata } from 'next'
49	
50	export const metadata: Metadata = {
51	  title: 'My Blog',
52	  description: '...',
53	}
54	
55	export default function Layout() {}
56	```
57	
58	```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher
59	export const metadata = {
60	  title: 'My Blog',
61	  description: '...',
62	}
63	
64	export default function Layout() {}
65	```
66	
67	You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields).
68	
69	## Generated metadata
70	
71	You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post:
72

78	  searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }>
79	}
80	
81	export async function generateMetadata(
82	  { params, searchParams }: Props,
83	  parent: ResolvingMetadata
84	): Promise<Metadata> {

86

1 --- 2 title: Fetching Data 3 description: Learn how to fetch data and stream content that depends on data. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/guides/data-security 9 - app/api-reference/functions/fetch 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading 11 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging 12 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/taint 13 --- 14 15 This page will walk you through how you can fetch data in [Server and Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components), and how to [stream](#streaming) components that depend on data. 16 17 ## Fetching data 18 19 ### Server Components 20 21 You can fetch data in Server Components using any asynchronous I/O, such as: 22 23 1. The [`fetch` API](#with-the-fetch-api) 24 2. An [ORM or database](#with-an-orm-or-database) 25 3. Reading from the filesystem using Node.js APIs like `fs` 26 27 #### With the `fetch` API 28 29 To fetch data with the `fetch` API, turn your component into an asynchronous function, and await the `fetch` call. For example: 30 31 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 32 export default async function Page() { 33 const data = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog') 34 const posts = await data.json() 35 return ( 36
    37 {posts.map((post) => ( 38
  • {post.title}

41 ) 42 } 43 ``` 44 45 ```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 46 export default async function Page() { 47 const data = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog') 48 const posts = await data.json() 49 return ( 50
    51 {posts.map((post) => ( 52
  • {post.title}
    • 53 ))} 54
55 ) 56 } 57 ``` 58 59 > **Good to know:** 60 > 61 > - `fetch` responses are not cached by default. However, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) the route and the output will be cached for improved performance. If you'd like to opt into [dynamic rendering](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the `{ cache: 'no-store' }` option. See the [`fetch` API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch). 62 > - During development, you can log `fetch` calls for better visibility and debugging. See the [`logging` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging). 63 64 #### With an ORM or database 65 66 Since Server Components are rendered on the server, you can safely make database queries using an ORM or database client. Turn your component into an asynchronous function, and await the call: 67 68 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 69 import { db, posts } from '@/lib/db' 70 71 export default async function Page() { 72 const allPosts = await db.select().from(posts) 73 return ( 74

  • {post.title}

94 ) 95 } 96 ``` 97 98 ### Client Components 99 100 There are two ways to fetch data in Client Components, using: 101 102 1. React's [`use` API](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) 103 2. A community library like [SWR](https://swr.vercel.app/) or [React Query](https://tanstack.com/query/latest) 104 105 #### Streaming data with the `use` API 106 107 You can use React's [`use` API](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) to [stream](#streaming) data from the server to client. 1 --- 2 title: Linking and Navigating 3 description: Learn how the built-in navigation optimizations work, including prefetching, prerendering, and client-side navigation, and how to optimize navigation for dynamic routes and slow networks. 4 related: 5 links: 6 - app/api-reference/components/link 7 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading 8 - app/guides/prefetching 9 --- 10 11 In Next.js, routes are rendered on the server by default. This often means the client has to wait for a server response before a new route can be shown. Next.js comes with built-in [prefetching](#prefetching), [streaming](#streaming), and [client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions) ensuring navigation stays fast and responsive. 12 13 This guide explains how navigation works in Next.js and how you can optimize it for [dynamic routes](#dynamic-routes-without-loadingtsx) and [slow networks](#slow-networks). 14 15 ## How navigation works 16 17 To understand how navigation works in Next.js, it helps to be familiar with the following concepts: 18 19 - [Server Rendering](#server-rendering) 20 - [Prefetching](#prefetching) 21 - [Streaming](#streaming) 22 - [Client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions) 23 24 ### Server Rendering 25 26 In Next.js, [Layouts and Pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages) are [React Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default. On initial and subsequent navigations, the [Server Component Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#how-do-server-and-client-components-work-in-nextjs) is generated on the server before being sent to the client. 27 28 There are two types of server rendering, based on _when_ it happens: 29 30 - **Static Rendering (or Prerendering)** happens at build time or during [revalidation](/docs/app/getting-started/caching-and-revalidating) and the result is cached. 31 - **Dynamic Rendering** happens at request time in response to a client request. 32 33 The trade-off of server rendering is that the client must wait for the server to respond before the new route can be shown. Next.js addresses this delay by [prefetching](#prefetching) routes the user is likely to visit and performing [client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions). 34 35 > **Good to know**: HTML is also generated for the initial visit. 36 37 ### Prefetching 38 39 Prefetching is the process of loading a route in the background before the user navigates to it. This makes navigation between routes in your application feel instant, because by the time a user clicks on a link, the data to render the next route is already available client side. 40 41 Next.js automatically prefetches routes linked with the [`` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) when they enter the user's viewport. 42 43 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 44 import Link from 'next/link' 45 46 export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 47 return ( 48 49 50
51 {/* Prefetched when the link is hovered or enters the viewport */} 52 Blog 53 {/* No prefetching */} 54 Contact 55
56 {children} 57 58 59 ) 60 } 61 ``` 62 63 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 64 import Link from 'next/link' 65 66 export default function Layout() { 67 return ( 68 69 70
71 {/* Prefetched when the link is hovered or enters the viewport */} 72 Blog 73 {/* No prefetching */} 74 Contact 75
76 {children} 77 78 79 ) 80 } 81 ``` 82 83 How much of the route is prefetched depends on whether it's static or dynamic: 84 85 - **Static Route**: the full route is prefetched. 86 - **Dynamic Rout…(+14185)

1 --- 2 title: Server and Client Components 3 description: Learn how you can use React Server and Client Components to render parts of your application on the server or the client. 4 related: 5 title: Next Steps 6 description: Learn more about the APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/directives/use-client 9 --- 10 11 By default, layouts and pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components), which lets you fetch data and render parts of your UI on the server, optionally cache the result, and stream it to the client. When you need interactivity or browser APIs, you can use [Client Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client) to layer in functionality. 12 13 This page explains how Server and Client Components work in Next.js and when to use them, with examples of how to compose them together in your application. 14 15 ## When to use Server and Client Components? 16 17 The client and server environments have different capabilities. Server and Client components allow you to run logic in each environment depending on your use case. 18 19 Use **Client Components** when you need: 20 21 - [State](https://react.dev/learn/managing-state) and [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events). E.g. `onClick`, `onChange`. 22 - [Lifecycle logic](https://react.dev/learn/lifecycle-of-reactive-effects). E.g. `useEffect`. 23 - Browser-only APIs. E.g. `localStorage`, `window`, `Navigator.geolocation`, etc. 24 - [Custom hooks](https://react.dev/learn/reusing-logic-with-custom-hooks). 25 26 Use **Server Components** when you need: 27 28 - Fetch data from databases or APIs close to the source. 29 - Use API keys, tokens, and other secrets without exposing them to the client. 30 - Reduce the amount of JavaScript sent to the browser. 31 - Improve the [First Contentful Paint (FCP)](https://web.dev/fcp/), and stream content progressively to the client. 32 33 For example, the `` component is a Server Component that fetches data about a post, and passes it as props to the `` which handles client-side interactivity. 34 35 ```tsx filename="app/[id]/page.tsx" highlight={1,17} switcher 36 import LikeButton from '@/app/ui/like-button' 37 import { getPost } from '@/lib/data' 38 39 export default async function Page({ 40 params, 41 }: { 42 params: Promise<{ id: string }> 43 }) { 44 const { id } = await params 45 const post = await getPost(id) 46 47 return ( 48
49
50

{post.title}

51 {/* ... */} 52 53
54
55 ) 56 } 57 ``` 58 59 ```jsx filename="app/[id]/page.js" highlight={1,12} switcher 60 import LikeButton from '@/app/ui/like-button' 61 import { getPost } from '@/lib/data' 62 63 export default async function Page({ params }) { 64 const post = await getPost(params.id) 65 66 return ( 67
68
69

{post.title}

70 {/* ... */} 71 72
73
74 ) 75 } 76 ``` 77 78 ```tsx filename="app/ui/like-button.tsx" highlight={1} switcher 79 'use client' 80 81 import { useState } from 'react' 82 83 export default function LikeButton({ likes }: { likes: number }) { 84 // ... 85 } 86 ``` 87 88 ```jsx filename="app/ui/like-button.js" highlight={1} switcher 89 'use client' 90 91 import { useState } from 'react' 92 93 export default function LikeButton({ likes }) { 94 // ... 95 } 96 ``` 97 98 ## How do Server and Client Components work in Next.js? 99 100 ### On the server 101 102 On the server, Next.js uses React's APIs to orchestrate rendering. The rendering work is split into chunks, by individual route segments ([layouts and pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages)): 103 104 - **Server Components** are rendered into a special data format called the React Server Component Payload (RSC Payload). 105 - **Clie…(+21964)
